nativeRange.habitat: Rapid-flowing, well-oxygenated mountain streams over boulders and gravel beds; periphytic algae (aufwuchs) and biofilm on rock surfaces

decorPreferences: water-worn boulders and rounded river rocks for grazing surfaces
strong unidirectional flow (10×+ turnover per hour)
Anubias and Microsorum anchored to rockwork
bright lighting to promote biofilm and algae growth

diet.notes: Specialist aufwuchs grazer: forages constantly on periphytic algae, diatoms, filamentous cyanobacteria, and biofilm scraped from rock surfaces. Supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking pellets, blanched vegetables (spinach, kale, zucchini), and occasional live or frozen foods (Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm). Algae-covered rock surfaces are essential for long-term health; fish will decline without adequate biofilm.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches of different genera
small plecos adapted to cooler, high-flow conditions
avoidWith: other Pseudogastromyzon (territorial with conspecifics in small tanks; keep in larger groups or separate)
slow-moving or delicate fish intolerant of strong flow

summary: Myers’ Hillstream Loach is a compact Chinese hillstream specialist (5–6 cm) from Guangdong and Hong Kong, prized for its torpedo-flat body and perpetual algae-grazing on rockwork. Demands cool water (18–24°C), high flow, and exceptional oxygenation; best kept in groups of six or more.
careNotes: Myers’ Hillstream Loach (Pseudogastromyzon myersi) is endemic to the fast-flowing streams of Guangdong Province and Hong Kong, where it lives permanently adhered to boulders and cobbles in highly oxygenated, cool water. Replicating this environment is the central challenge: water temperature must be kept between 18 and 24°C (a chiller or basement installation is often required in warm climates), turnover should exceed 10× the tank volume per hour, and dissolved oxygen must remain very high. A long-footprint tank (minimum 75 cm) with the bulk of floor space covered by smooth river stones and boulders is strongly preferred over a deep or heavily planted setup. Bright lighting is beneficial as it drives biofilm and algae growth on rock surfaces — this aufwuchs is the species’ primary food source and must be present for the fish to thrive. Supplement with algae wafers and sinking spirulina-based foods daily; occasional frozen Daphnia or Artemia can be offered. Males are territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped loaches if space is limited; keeping a group of six or more in an appropriately sized tank typically distributes aggression. The species is an adept climber — a tightly fitting lid with no gaps is mandatory. Water quality must be pristine: perform regular partial water changes and avoid any accumulation of nitrate above ~10–15 ppm. Soft to moderately soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) matches the low-mineral hillstream habitat. This species is not suited to typical tropical community setups due to its cool temperature requirement.
breedingNotes: Bred in captivity by specialist keepers. Males dig shallow spawning depressions in fine gravel and attract females through fin displays; eggs are deposited and then covered. Incubation varies with temperature (approximately 4–14 days). Fry are small and require infusoria or green water initially, transitioning to micro-algae and fine powdered foods. No parental care is provided after spawning. Conditioning with a varied diet including live foods improves spawning success.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (50–60 mm), temperature 18.9–23.9°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 1–12°H, native range (Hong Kong and Guangdong), substrate recommendations, flow requirements (>10×/hr turnover), diet (biofilm specialist), grouping (6+), and breeding notes (gravel-nest spawning, 4–14 days incubation).

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (6 cm TL), native range (Guangdong Province, China; Hong Kong), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), habitat (rapid-flowing freshwater streams), diet (periphytic algae and detritus on rock surfaces). Common length 2.9 cm SL noted; 6 cm TL used as adult maximum per species brief.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Confirmed temperature 20–23.8°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness max 12 dH, adult size 6 cm, high-flow requirement, diet (flake, wafers, bloodworm, algae), and spawning behaviour (male digs hole, fin display, covers eggs). Synonym Hemimyzon myersi noted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General hillstream loach trade care guide. Confirmed temperature range 20–27°C for the group, diet (algae wafers, gel foods, frozen spirulina brine shrimp, blanched vegetables), tight-fitting lid requirement, and compatibility with peaceful community fish. Note: guide focuses primarily on Sewellia lineolata; parameters cross-checked against SeriouslyFish for P. myersi specifics.

additional.name: GBIF — Pseudogastromyzon myersi Herre, 1932
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic authority (Herre, 1932), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), and geographic occurrence data (Guangdong Province, China; Hong Kong). Used to verify classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Males dig gravel spawning depressions and court females through fin displays; eggs are covered after fertilisation. Incubation 4–14 days depending on temperature. Fry require infusoria and microalgae initially. Specialist-level setup with pristine water quality and high flow needed for conditioning and spawning.
